texts.text: 1. Introduction

texts.label: text
texts.orig: As the use of fossil fuels increases, greenhouse gas emissions increase, thereby worsening global warming. To prevent the increasing trend of global warming, global leaders have signed the Paris agreement (2015), which is focused on reducing greenhouse gas emissions. Carbon dioxide, which is mainly emitted from large point sources such as coal or gas- fi red power plants and steel mills, accounts for the largest portion of greenhouse gases. Among these sources, coal- fi red power plants are the major CO2 emission sources, contributing approximately 26% of the energy-related CO2 emissions [1].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Solvent absorption is generally accepted as the most feasible technique for CO2 capture from large CO2 emission sources [2 e 4]. The challenge faced by amine-based wet absorption technology is the need to develop a low-energy and high-ef fi ciency process for commercialization [3,5,6]. Thus, solvent development is essential for overcoming this problem.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Aqueous alkanol amines or their blends are used as solvents for acid gas removal in most amine scrubbing processes. In this study, we selected six polyamines with three amine groups that have suffi ciently high boiling temperatures and evaluated their potentials as CO2 capture solvents by investigating the CO2 vapor e liquid equilibrium, mass transfer rate, viscosity, and thermal stability. Among the tested polyamines, two were chosen and mixed with 2-(butylamino)ethanol and piperazine (PZ) to compensate for the inferior properties of each polyamine, and their properties were investigated. As a result, aqueous 2,6,10-trimethyl-2,6,10-triazaundecane mixed with a small amount of PZ showed a great potential as a new CO2 capture solvent in that it has a quite high absorption rate and thermal stability and a low reboiler duty.
texts.text: Aqueous alkanol amines or their blends are used as solvents for acid gas removal in most amine scrubbing processes. In this study, we selected six polyamines with three amine groups that have suffi ciently high boiling temperatures and evaluated their potentials as CO2 capture solvents by investigating the CO2 vapor e liquid equilibrium, mass transfer rate, viscosity, and thermal stability. Among the tested polyamines, two were chosen and mixed with 2-(butylamino)ethanol and piperazine (PZ) to compensate for the inferior properties of each polyamine, and their properties were investigated. As a result, aqueous 2,6,10-trimethyl-2,6,10-triazaundecane mixed with a small amount of PZ showed a great potential as a new CO2 capture solvent in that it has a quite high absorption rate and thermal stability and a low reboiler duty.

Among many different amines, alkanol monoamines easily meet the previously mentioned demands and have been researched extensively. Polyamine molecules can react with more than two CO2 molecules; thus, their ability to absorb CO2 may be higher than that of monoamines. texts.label: text
texts.orig: The amine group can be classi fi ed into primary, secondary, tertiary [9], and hindered. Primary and secondary amine groups form carbamates with CO2 and have high absorption rates and heats of reaction. The tertiary amine group forms bicarbonate when reacted with CO2 and has a low absorption rate and heat of reaction. The hindered amine group forms carbamates. However, with its instability, it has a relatively lower absorption rate and heat of reaction than primary and secondary amines. Amine groups in a polyamine can be any combination of primary, secondary, tertiary [9], or hindered amines. Their properties and potential as CO2 capture solvents may vary widely depending on the speci fi c structure of the polyamine.

texts.orig: monoethanolamine (MEA) and PZ are typical single amines for CO2 capture solvent [7], and aMDEA, a mixture of methyldiethanolamine (MDEA) and PZ, is a representative mixed amine solvent. Aqueous MEA is regarded as a benchmark solvent owing to its cheap price and long-time use in industry, despite its relatively high reboiler duty (3.3 e 3.6 GJ/tCO2) and oxidative and thermal instabilities. PZ has superior properties compared to those of MEA in terms of reboiler duty, oxidative and thermal stabilities, and vaporization loss [7]. KS-1, a mixed amine developed by Mitsubishi Heavy Industries, is known to have a low reboiler duty (2.5 GJ/tCO2) and high solvent stability [10]. It is currently applied in a 240MWeCO2capture plant in Texas. Recently, water-lean solvents such as CO2Bol and NAS [11 e 13] are actively researched since the reboiler duty is expected to be very low (2.0 GJ/t-CO2 or less).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this study, properties of six selected aqueous polyamines and their mixtures with some selected mono- and di-amines were investigated, and their potentials as CO2 capture solvents were evaluated. Polyamines investigated in this study are triamines that have the same nonane backbone with carbon atoms at the 1, 5, and 9 positions replaced by nitrogen atoms, each of which formed primary, secondary, or tertiary amine groups. Studies on polyamines with this structure are rare except for 3,3 0 -iminobis(N,Ndimethylpropylamine) (IBDMPA or TMBPA) [27 e 29] and N,Ndimethyldipropylene-triamine (DMAPAPA) [30], resulting in a lack of information on the solvent properties. As far as the authors know, there has been no systematic study comparing the potential as CO2 capture solvents to a family of homolog polyamines.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: CO2 solubility was measured using a continuous fl ow bubbling reactor (CFBR). The CFBR can be operated in either mediumpressure mode or low-pressure mode. The medium and low pressures mean CO2 equilibrium partial pressure (EPP) from 1 to 20 kPa and CO2 EPP below 1 kPa, respectively.
texts.text: CO2 solubility was measured using a continuous fl ow bubbling reactor (CFBR). The CFBR can be operated in either mediumpressure mode or low-pressure mode. The medium and low pressures mean CO2 equilibrium partial pressure (EPP) from 1 to 20 kPa and CO2 EPP below 1 kPa, respectively.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The CFBR system is depicted in Fig. 2. Seven N2 e CO2 mixed gas cylinders with different CO2 mole fractions from 0 to 20% were connected to the feed line of the reactor through individual mass fl ow controllers (MFCs, EL-Flow F-201CV from Bronkhorst, accuracy of ± 0.005 SLM). A double-walled glass vessel with a solvent capacity of 50 mL was used as the reactor. The reactor temperature was maintained constant using water circulating between the reactor jacket and the heating bath (NCB-1200 from Eyela, accuracy of ± 0.1  C). Because of negligible pressure drop in the tubing and peripherals, the reactor pressure was assumed to be equal to the atmospheric pressure measured using a barometric pressure transducer (Omega, PX409, 88 e 110 kPa, accuracy of 0.08%).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A N2 e CO2 mixed gas was purged through a glass bubbler into the solvent at a fl ow rate of 1 standard liter per minute (SLM). The gas discharged from the reactor passes through a condenser to recover water and amine vapor, and then passes through a desiccant unit (CaCl2 column) to completely remove moisture. Three NDIR CO2 sensors with different spans (30%, 1% and 0.1%), all from Edinburgh Gascard NG (accuracy of ± 2% of span), were installed in the gas exit line. Only one of them with appropriate measurement range was switched to the gas exit line.

texts.orig: Water loss from the CFBR was calculated using the temperature measurement at the condenser exit with the assumption that the exit gas is saturated with water. Whenever the total amount of water loss reached 0.5 mL, make-up water was injected. The CO2 loading was obtained using the CO2 concentration measurement. A precise deconvolution technique described in Jung and Lee [5] was applied to compensate for the empty volume dynamics. Otherwise, the CFBR experiment is similar to other groups ' CFBR experiments [15,16,18,21,31]. We brie fl y describe the key features of the experimental technique.

texts.orig: To demonstrate the experimental method, we show an experimental result (CO2 mole fraction at the reactor outlet) and calculated CO2 loading at 40  C for MEA at 30 mass% in Fig. 3. By integrating the difference of the CO2 mole fraction, yCO2 , between the feed gas and the outlet gas, the CO2 absorption (or desorption) amount can be known, and the CO2 loading, ldg , over time can be calculated. The CO2 EPP corresponding to a steady state ldg deviates from the CO2 mole fraction of the feed gas due to the existence of water vapor. As is discussed in a later section, the CO2 loading is in good agreement with literature values [32 e 35]. It required less than 4 h to complete a set of experiments for fi ve CO2 EPPs at one temperature.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: At low CO2 EPPs, the time required to reach an equilibrium state becomes too long as revealed in Fig. 3 because of diminished driving force for mass transfer. The required experimental time was longer than 1 h for the feed gas with 1 kPa of CO2 PP, and the situation is aggravated at a lower CO2 PP. To cope with this problem, the measurement technique proposed by Dugas (2009) [36] to measure the CO2 solubility and mass transfer rate using a WWC in a low-pressure region [36] was applied to the CFBR. In this technique, a preloaded solvent should be prepared fi rst, and the corresponding CO2 EPP is determined through a trial-and-error procedure. Several repetitions or less were enough to attain an equilibrium state within a reasonable tolerance. The loading of a loaded solvent was measured using a scale (Quintix3102-1S from Sartorious AG) with the accuracy of ± 0.01 g.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A modifi ed wetted wall column (m-WWC) unit was used to measure the CO2 absorption rate and viscosity of the solvent together while continuously increasing the CO2 loading [5]. The mWWC unit has a small-volume (120 mL) solvent container, and a liquid metering pump (OM006 from FLOMEC, accuracy of ± 1% of span ( ± 3 mL/min)) circulates the solvent between the WWC and the container so that the CO2 loading is continuously increased. In addition, a differential pressure (DP) transmitter (DWS from Sensys, accuracy of ± 0.5% of span ( ± 0.5 kPa) installed across a straight segment of the solvent circulation tubing. The viscosity was calculated using the DP measurement based on the Hagen e Poiseuille equation after its coef fi cient was adjusted using measurements from a viscometer (Brook fi eld DV II þ Pro).

texts.orig: 2.3. Thermal degradation
texts.text: 2.3. Thermal degradation

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Tightly sealed tube reactors made of SUS 316 were used to investigate the solvent thermal degradation at high temperature. After the solvent with rich CO2 loading was charged and a carbon steel specimen was inserted, the reactor was placed in an oven at 120  C. Thermal degradation was analyzed after three days. Triethylene glycol at approximately 1 mass% was added to the solvent as the internal standard for gas chromatograph (GC, Agilent 7890B, column: CP-Sil 8CB)) analysis [27,38]. No calibration curve was generated for the peak area, but instead the degree of thermal degeneration was expressed by the change in peak area.

texts.text: 3. Materials

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Solvent evaluations were performed on three aqueous monoamines, six aqueous polyamines, and four blends of a polyamine and a monoamine or PZ as shown in Table 1. The amine contents of all tested solvents were 30 mass%. Three monoamines, MEA, BAE, and IPAE, are alkanol amines and were chosen to validate the reliability of the experimental methods and to use as promoters of selected polyamines. MEA is a primary amine; BAE a secondary amine; IPAE is also a secondary amine with steric hindrance. In addition to these monoamines, PZ, a diamine, was also used as a promotor for polyamine P6.

P2 has two primary amine groups and one tertiary amine group and so on.

texts.orig: when reacting with CO2. P2 is a polyamine containing primary, secondary, and tertiary amines. The heat of reaction is similar to that of MEA; however, the cyclic capacity is larger than that of P1, which is likely the combined effect of the three different amine groups. P3 has a secondary amine at the center and two tertiary amines on both ends. The characteristics of the combined secondary and tertiary amines are strongly exhibited by the largest cyclic capacity and relatively small heat of reaction. P4 has a similar structure to P1 with the secondary amine at the center replaced by a tertiary amine. The CO2 solubility curve appears to be a shift of the solubility curve of P1 to the left. P5 has a tertiary amine at the center and secondary amines at both ends. Its estimated heat of reaction is between those of primary amine-containing polyamines and P6, which has only tertiary amines; thus, P5 is considered to form both bicarbonate and carbamate in the operating region, like a secondary amine. In Fig. 5 (c) and (d), P5 shows a higher molar CO2 solubility than P3 in the low P * CO 2 region; however, the situation is reversed in the high P * CO 2 region. This means that carbamate formation is active with P5 in the low P * CO 2 region owing to the two secondary amines. As P * CO 2 was increased, bicarbonate production became active but not enough to suf fi ciently augment the cyclic capacity.

texts.orig: P6 is solely composed of tertiary amines; it can thus produce only bicarbonate and has the lowest heat of reaction but not a high enough cyclic capacity over the considered nominal operating region. However, the slope of the CO2 solubility curve is the smallest among the six polyamines at P * CO 2 of approximately 3 kPa or above. Therefore, the cyclic capacity can be signi fi cantly large in a CO2 absorption process that treats a feed gas with a high CO2 partial pressure.

The exception is P3, which shows approximately 76 g-CO2/ kg-solv of cyclic capacity.

texts.orig: Using the CO2 solubility and heat of reaction data, the reboiler duty in the standard absorber e stripper con fi guration was calculated using the short-cut method [26]. In the calculation, the heat capacity of the solvent was fi xed at 3.7 kJ/kg K, which is the value for 30 mass% MEA, and the rich loading was fi xed at the nominal value de fi ned in section 2.4. In addition, it was assumed that the minimum temperature difference (MTD) in the main heat exchanger was 10  C, and the stripper pressure was 2 bar.

texts.orig: The results are depicted in Fig. 6. P3 has the lowest reboiler duty of 2.83 GJ/t-CO2, which is approximately 15% less than that of MEA (3.33 GJ/t-CO2). This is due to the least sensible heat caused by the highest cyclic capacity among the tested amines and the moderate value of the heat of reaction at approximately 70 kJ/mol-CO2. Thus, the sum of the heat of reaction and the latent heat, which are in a trade-off relation, was the smallest. texts.label: text
texts.orig: Fig. 8(a) and (b) show the absorption rate over the entire CO2 loading and the nominal operating region, respectively, for all considered amines except for PZ. For MEA, measured values by Dugas (2009) [36] are compared to validate the performance of the m-WWC. In Table 4, initial (fresh state) and average absorption rates are listed for all considered amines. The absorption rates in the fresh state increased in the following order: P6, IPAE, P3, BAE, P2, MEA, P4, P5, P1, and PZ. However, the average absorption rates over the operating region, which were calculated according to Eq. (2), increased in the following order: P6, P1, P4, IPAE, P3, P5, P2, MEA, BAE, and PZ. Amines with high absorption rates in the fresh state do not necessarily represent high absorption rates in the CO2loaded operating region. PZ, as a renowned absorption rate promoter, has an excellent absorption rate regardless of other factors.

texts.orig: 4.1.5. Thermal degradation
texts.text: 4.1.5. Thermal degradation

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Table 5 shows the experimental results for thermal degradation of the solvents. Thermal degradation of the polyamines, except for P6, was found to be severe compared to that of the monoamines. In particular, P4 and P5, which possess a tertiary amine at the center, were observed to be weaker than other amines that have a secondary amine at the center. One exception was P6. It exhibited thermal degradation of only approximately one third of the thermal degradation of MEA.

texts.orig: 4.2. Mixed amines
texts.text: 4.2. Mixed amines

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Among the tested polyamines, P3 was determined to be the most ef fi cient in terms of performance characteristics, such as cyclic capacity and reboiler duty, and P6 was determined to be the most resistant to thermal degradation. However, these amines have a drawback in that the absorption rate is not high enough. In particular, P6 has too a low absorption rate. To overcome this drawback, four binary amine mixtures were designed by choosing another from the monoamines or PZ so that one of them acts as an absorption rate promoter. BAE, which has the highest average absorption rate among the monoamines and PZ were used as the rate promotors. The mixed amines were designed as follows: (B1: P3 20% þ BAE 10%), (B2: P6 20% þ BAE 10%), and (B3: P6 20% þ PZ 10%).

texts.orig: Fig. 10 shows the CO2 solubilities of the mixed amines. The raw experimental data are provided in Table A2 in the Appendix. Table 6 summarizes the cyclic capacities, heats of reaction, and estimated reboiler duties. B1 retained its cyclic capacity, heat of reaction, and reboiler duty at a level similar to P3, the main substance. This is due to BAE, which was added as an absorption rate enhancer but has a low heat of reaction and relatively high cyclic capacity as a secondary amine. B2 and B3 have similar values for their heats of reaction and CO2 capacities, which increased by 20% and 10%, respectively, compared to those of P6. The minimum reboiler duties were calculated to be lower than that of P6 (see Fig. 11).

The thermal degradation of PZ in the mixed amine was relatively smaller than in other tested amines but much worse than single PZ.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 5. Conclusions
texts.text: 5. Conclusions

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this study, the potential of polyamines as CO2 capture solvents was investigated for single aqueous amines and mixed aqueous amines with promoters. The polyamines investigated in this study were limited to triamines with the nonane backbone, where methyl groups at both ends and C e H at the center were replaced by amine groups. The four mixed amines were formed as mixtures of one of three polyamines, designated P1, P3 (IBDMPA) and P6, and one of IPAE, BAE and PZ. All amine solvents were prepared at 30 mass% and showed viscosities below 7 cP at 40  C, which is low enough not to cause any design and operational issues.
texts.text: In this study, the potential of polyamines as CO2 capture solvents was investigated for single aqueous amines and mixed aqueous amines with promoters. The polyamines investigated in this study were limited to triamines with the nonane backbone, where methyl groups at both ends and C e H at the center were replaced by amine groups. The four mixed amines were formed as mixtures of one of three polyamines, designated P1, P3 (IBDMPA) and P6, and one of IPAE, BAE and PZ. All amine solvents were prepared at 30 mass% and showed viscosities below 7 cP at 40  C, which is low enough not to cause any design and operational issues.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Among the six tested polyamines, P6 (2,6,10-trimethyl-2,6,10triazaundecane) had exceptional strength against thermal degradation, while the other fi ve did not, and fairly large cyclic capacity, especially in the region above a CO2 EPP of 3 kPa. With only tertiary amines, however the CO2 absorption rate is very low. To improve the absorption rate, mixed amines were formulated by adding promoters. Among them, B3 that is mixed with PZ at 10 mass% showed the fastest absorption rate and the best thermal stability. Also, the reboiler duty was estimated to be the lowest among all considered single and mixed amines. In this respect, B3 is considered a promising candidate for CO2-absorbing solvents. Optimizing the composition of P6 and PZ is a sure way to further improve the properties of B3.

However, lean and rich loading values in real processes may deviate largely from these values. Therefore, process simulation and pilot test should be followed for more accurate assessment of selected candidate solvents.

texts.orig: For validation of the proposed method, experiments were performed with 15.0 and 30.0 mass% of aqueous MEA solvent and 14.7 and 30.1 mass% of aqueous PZ solvent over the pressure range of 0.01 e 2.1 kPa for MEA and 0.02 e 5.1 kPa for PZ, respectively. The results of this study agreed well with the literature data and validated the reliability of the proposed method.
